WHO I AM
About Me

I'm a Mechanical Engineering student at BYU's Ira A. Fulton College of Engineering, graduating May 2026. My work spans automation engineering, compliant mechanism design, advanced surface modeling, and R&D for humanitarian applications.

I've contributed to cockpit hardware at Korry Electronics, built robotic systems at Ultradent, designed a compact folding quadcopter, and developed low-cost prosthetics for underserved communities in Ecuador.

TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE
Potential Fields Path Planning

A robot arm navigation algorithm using virtual attractive and repulsive forces to reach a target while avoiding obstacles — implemented in Python with custom singularity recovery.

⛰️
How It Works

Think of obstacles as high mountain peaks — joints want to roll away from them. The goal acts like a valley, always attracting the end effector. The arm navigates by following the combined gradient of these force fields.

🔁
Repulsion (Non-linear)

Repulsion increases exponentially as a joint approaches an obstacle. Each joint has an assigned repulsion region — conservative by default, tunable to allow the arm to get closer or maintain more clearance.

U_r += k_re × (0.1/dist − 1/obst_rad) × grad_η
🎯
Attraction (Linear)

Attraction to the goal is linear — a gain multiplied by the XYZ error vector. Both attraction and repulsion are multiplied by separate tuning gains to balance their influence and smooth the path.

F_attract = K × error
⚡
Singularity Recovery

When the end effector hasn't improved its distance to goal in 3 seconds, the arm is commanded back to the zero position and extends in a random radial direction to escape the singularity and retry.

Tuning Parameters
  • Attraction gain K — scales pull strength toward the goal
  • Repulsion gain k_re — scales push strength away from obstacles
  • Obstacle radius obst_rad — defines the influence boundary of each obstacle
  • Max step size — caps joint movement per iteration to prevent large jumps
  • Joint repulsion regions — conservative safety zones around each joint sphere
